data.title: Alexandria Real Estate: Red Pill Blue Pill
data.description:
We recently explained our position on Alexandria Real Estate Equities Inc. (ARE). The summary below covers the take-home message.

ARE does have one of the best balance sheets as far as length of debt maturities. But a deteriorating outlook more than makes up for it in the long run. But that long length might allow this story to continue for a few more years. It is also possible that things change after the next election. But at present, there is no reason to get involved outside of trading rallies.

Source: Why You Should Still Stay Out Of Alexandria Real Estate

While the bulls might have been surprised with the 10% drop today, we absolutely weren't. We go over the key part of the earnings release, which likely panicked a few more cheerleaders.

Q1 2026

The press release started off ok. There was none of the "beating expectations" thing that you see with most companies. But at least the funds from operations (FFO) were about in line with analyst expectations. The earlier slides also showcased the "strong" leasing volumes with an even higher amount expected in Q2 2026.

We used "strong" in quotes because it is not actually strong. ARE has 60 million square feet of properties with an average lease term of 7.5 years.

So to keep things at the status quo, ARE needs to be renewing leases at about 8 million square feet a year. 60 million divided by 7.5. This does not include their new projects, which will require some serious leasing efforts by themselves. In other words, ARE needs a quarterly run rate of 2 million square feet of leasing. You can see why we thought Q1 2026 and Q2 2026 were fairly bad.

Moving on, ARE reaffirmed its newly acquired religion to reduce non-income-producing properties, an act that we equate with playing violin on the decks of the Titanic.

But that might have still brought cheer to the bulls, who have been eager to grasp at any straws that they can find. Unfortunately, it got pretty bad, pretty quick from there. The key number that likely caused a double take for the serious, discerning investor was the rental rate change. Sure, ARE is signing leases, but what are they getting for these leases? Turns out, a lot less.

The 15% decline is another splash of ice-cold water on their faces. This was quickly followed by the occupancy at 87.7%

As we have previously said, 80% is enough to take most, if not all, REITs into restructuring. These numbers are downright scary. We can also examine them relative to the last quarter, and that actually makes them scarier. Note that Q4 2025 had some bad rental changes, and those were significantly worse than the whole year 2025 and in a different zone than the whole year 2024.

The 15% decline seen in Q1 2026 is at another level. Similarly, the occupancy deterioration is telling you that the market is breaking under the surface.

Now all of this could be excused as a bad quarter. But ARE did not leave it there. They downgraded their outlook on all three areas that matter most to investors. Ending occupancy as of December 31, 2026, was taken down a notch. This is something we have been harping on about. In our view, management is still underestimating the sheer impact of the current administration's policies towards life science buildings.

If the occupancy was bad, the net operating income (NOI) and lease renewal downgrades threw some extra salt into those wounds. We will note that ARE was expecting a laughable negative 2% to positive 6% rental change just one quarter back for the full year 2026. The new numbers are now getting in the right zone, but they will likely head even lower.

Verdict

Of course, bulls might point to the fact that the FFO guidance wasn't moved down. Our take on that is the kitchen sink quarter likely had some buffer built in to absorb some headwinds on FFO. But that buffer has likely been eroded with this quarter's numbers. The stock is now breaking lower and taking out all bastions of support as people start pricing in the inevitable credit rating cut.

Can anything save ARE? Well, we don't believe so. The damage from extreme overbuilding alongside running into the most hostile policies at the US administration level has sealed this case.

Our opinion is that we are still seeing the very early effects from these changes. The end result will likely be a wipeout, though the very long-term nature of ARE's debt makes the timing harder to ascertain. As Morpheus would have put it about AE going under, "It is not a matter of faith. It is a matter of time." We would get out of this "illusion of value" stock by taking the red pill.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it and sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Broadcom: Investors Are Pricing In Perfection
data.description:
About three months ago, in late January 2026, I wrote my last article about Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), and I was very cautious about the stock – similar to most other semiconductor companies, which I consider overvalued at this point. In the conclusion of my last Broadcom article, I wrote:

In the end, I remain cautious about Broadcom. It certainly is possible for the company to grow 19% annually for the next 10 years, followed by 6% growth until perpetuity, and therefore be at least fairly valued. However, in my opinion, we can also make the case for growth rates slowing down more abruptly than many expect right now, and investors could suddenly be faced with steep declines and maybe have to wait for several years before the stock price returns to previous levels again.

However, like most other semiconductor companies, Broadcom continues to grow at a high pace. And since my last article was published, Broadcom increased about 27% in value, while the S&P 500 increased only 2.5%

Nevertheless, I will argue once again that Broadcom is a bit overvalued and a rather risky investment at this point. Of course, we have to acknowledge that Broadcom is reporting great results at this point and growing at a high pace. But the stock price is already indicating that investors are extrapolating high-growth rates over a very long time – and this makes the stock a risky investment.

First Quarter Results

On March 4, 2026, Broadcom reported its first-quarter results for fiscal 2026 and beat estimates for revenue as well as earnings per share. Net revenue increased from $14,916 million in Q1/25 to $19,311 million – representing 29.5% year-over-year top-line growth. Operating income increased 36.8% year-over-year from $6,260 million in the same quarter last year to $8,563 million in this quarter. Diluted net income per share also increased from $1.14 in Q1/25 to $1.50 in Q1/26, resulting in 31.6% year-over-year bottom line growth. Finally, free cash flow increased from $6,013 million in the same quarter last year to $8,010 million in this quarter – resulting in 33.2% year-over-year growth for this important metric.

Bullish Scenario

Without much doubt, Broadcom is reporting great results and growing at a high pace. Analysts are also expecting these high growth rates to last for several years to come. Until fiscal 2031, earnings per share are expected to grow at a CAGR of 31.7%, and revenue is expected to grow even with a CAGR of 33.1%.

Aside from extremely high-growth rates for the top and bottom line, we should also point out that analysts are constantly getting more optimistic and are raising targets for earnings per share as well as revenue for the quarters and years to come. Especially in the last few months, these earnings revisions seem to accelerate.

Management itself is only providing guidance for the second quarter but is also very optimistic and assuming growth rates to accelerate. Revenue is expected to be around $22 billion, which would result in 47% year-over-year growth. And this would represent a further acceleration in top-line growth. When excluding the acquisition, which led to high revenue growth rates in 2024, we see growth constantly accelerating since 2020.

While Broadcom does not provide mid-term or long-term guidance, the company made an extremely optimistic statement for its AI revenue from chips for 2027 during the last earnings call:

Our visibility in 2027 has dramatically improved. Today, in fact, we have line of sight to achieve AI revenue from chips, just chips, in excess of $100 billion in 2027. We have also secured the supply chain required to achieve this.

When asked about this high forecast by an analyst during the earnings call, CEO Hock Tan doubled down and stated that revenue will be significantly in excess of $100 billion:

Now to clarify your first part, Blayne, when I say we forecast, we have a line of sight that our revenue in '27 will be significantly in excess of $100 billion. I'm focusing on the fact that these are pretty much all based on chips, whether they are XPUs, whether they are switch chips, DSPs, these are silicon content we're talking about.

Additionally, analysts also have high price targets for the stock – implying upside potential in the years to come. On average, analysts have a price target of $477.50 for the stock, with some analysts having price targets as high as $630. Additionally, when calculating with very optimistic assumptions (32% growth for the next ten years – in line with analysts’ assumptions mentioned above), we get an intrinsic value around $620 for the stock.

Growth rates of 32% for the next ten years seem to be optimistic, as growth rates in the last ten years were “only” 25.1% for revenue and 25.7% for earnings per share, but one might argue that growth is accelerating – especially due to AI semiconductor sales.

Bearish Scenario

Here we are at one of the biggest problems the company (and stock price) is facing, in my opinion. Let’s look at the segment results for starters. Broadcom is reporting in two different segments – Semiconductor Solutions and Infrastructure Software. When looking at fiscal 2025, Broadcom generated $63.9 billion in revenue, and $27.0 billion in revenue stemmed from Infrastructure Software and $36.9 billion in revenue stemmed from Semiconductor Solutions.

When looking at growth rates in fiscal 2025, Infrastructure Software sales increased 26% year-over-year, and Semiconductor Solutions increased 22% year-over-year. This seems like a balanced picture with both segments growing at a high pace. However, in the first quarter for fiscal 2026, the picture looks different. Semiconductor Solutions increased its revenue by 52.4% year-over-year to $12,515 million, while Infrastructure Software increased its revenue by only 1.4% year-over-year to $6,796 million.

Additionally, when looking at the Semiconductor Solutions segment, the company is differentiating between the AI semiconductor business and the non-AI semiconductor business. The non-AI semiconductor business generated $4.1 billion in revenue, basically flat year-over-year (and in line with guidance). For the second quarter, management is expecting about 4% growth year-over-year. The AI semiconductor business, on the other hand, reported extremely high growth rates and is expecting growth to accelerate in the second quarter. During the last earnings call, management commented:

This robust growth was driven by AI semiconductor revenue, which grew 106% year-on-year to $8.4 billion, way above our outlook. In Q2, this momentum accelerates, and we expect semiconductor revenue to be $14.8 billion, up 76% year-on-year. Driving this is AI revenue growth, which will accelerate very sharply to 140% year-on-year to $10.7 billion.

Of course, it is always better when several different segments of a business contribute to growth, but I would not go so far as to claim it is a problem that the AI semiconductor business is solely responsible for growth in this quarter. However, the fact that Broadcom (similar to NVIDIA) is depending on only a few customers to drive growth and contribute a huge part of revenue might be a problem. I already mentioned this problem of high customer concentration in my October 2025 article. Back then, I wrote:

In the case of B2B businesses, a concentration on only a few customers is not unusual. However, the risk remains that a single customer can lead to a steep revenue decline and therefore have a huge impact on the overall performance of the business. Being responsible for a huge part of overall revenue is also leading to pricing power for these customers, and as Broadcom investors, we don’t want a customer to have pricing power over Broadcom.

In its last 10-K, the company estimated that the top 5 end customers account for about 40% of total sales (when combining the different sales channels) – the same percentage as in fiscal 2024.

Aside from the high concentration on only five customers, we should also question whether management’s assumptions for fiscal 2027 (at least $100 billion in AI revenue) are reasonable. In fiscal 2025, AI semiconductor revenue was $20.2 billion, and to reach at least $100 billion in revenue in fiscal 2027, revenue must grow at least 122% annually for fiscal 2026 and fiscal 2027. I do not claim this is impossible, but these are extremely high growth rates, and there is a high risk that Broadcom will report lower growth rates in the years to come.

In my last NVIDIA article, I made a similar argument for NVIDIA. CEO Jen-Hsun Huang also made extremely optimistic predictions for the years to come and assumed as much as $4 trillion in AI infrastructure spending by 2030. I argued that AI infrastructure spending would make up about 10% of US GDP – a number that seems extremely unlikely. I also pointed out that telecom spending was around $140 billion in 2000 and therefore about 1.35% of the GDP of $10.44 trillion back then.

This is actually quite similar to the current numbers. JPMorgan Chase is estimating that the hyperscalers spent about $416 billion on AI infrastructure in 2025, which is resulting in about 1.3% of current GDP.

JPMorgan Chase is seeing AI infrastructure spending still increasing in the years to come, but at a much lower pace. And we should be prepared that AI spending might also be close to its peak, and we can see a similar scenario as in 2000 and the following years. I know, such a scenario seems extremely unlikely, but this is almost always part of such cycles – only in hindsight does it seem reasonable.

Additionally, we can raise the question of how such factors as COT (customer-owned tooling) might be a problem. But when analysts asked about the topic during the earnings call, management quickly pointed out how difficult chip production is and what potential competitors would have to face:

(…) they face tremendous challenges. One is technology, which is technology as it relates to creating the silicon chips and particularly in XPUs that they need to do the computing and that is needed to optimize and run the -- train and inference on the workloads they produce out their LLM. It's -- that technology we talked about comes from -- comes in from different dimensions. You need the best silicon design team around.

You need cutting edge, really cutting-edge SerDes, very advanced packaging and most -- and just as much, you need to understand how to network clusters of them together. We've been doing this for 20 years, more than 20 years in silicon. And in this particular space today in generative AI, if you're trying to, as an LLM player, to do your own chip, you cannot afford to have a chip that is just good enough. You need the best chips that is around because you're competing against other LLM players.

And the CEO added that it might not be so difficult to design and produce a single chip, but producing these at scale is not so easy:

And one thing I add in there that is particularly unique to us, when you create a silicon, you really have to get it up and running in high volume in production very quickly, time to market. We are very, very experienced in doing that. Anybody can design a chip in a lab that works well. Can you produce 100,000 of those chips quickly at yields that you can afford? And we don't see too many players in the world that can do that.

Management is certainly right that neither the design nor the production of a chip is easy, and we are not talking about a product that everybody can make right away.

But Broadcom is also not producing its own chips, as it is a fabless company. And why shouldn’t major corporations – and we are talking about major corporations – not be able to have their chips manufactured by Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) like Broadcom does? Additionally, companies like Alibaba are already using their own chips, and while Alibaba is still acknowledging they can’t match the quality of NVIDIA, Google has been producing high-quality chips for several years.

We can see that the risk of several companies suddenly producing their own chips is rather low, but it is certainly a question worth discussing, as such a move would not be unprecedented.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

I already mentioned above that Broadcom could still be deeply undervalued when calculating with high growth rates (in line with analysts’ high growth assumptions). Nevertheless, Broadcom is expensive and trading almost in the top 5% of S&P 500 companies with the highest P/E ratio and P/FCF ratio. At the time of writing, Broadcom is trading for 82 times earnings and for 71 times free cash flow.

Instead, let’s provide another intrinsic value calculation. We will calculate with slightly different assumptions, and in my opinion, we have to take into account that the current high demand will at some point slow down. As always, we are calculating with a 10% discount rate (and this is important, as even a fairly valued stock will generate about 10% in annual returns and not the much higher returns many investors might be hoping for). Additionally, we are calculating with the last reported diluted shares outstanding (4,888 million) and the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $28,910 million.

That certainly seems optimistic, and the company is reporting a free cash flow of 42.3% in the last four quarters. While such an FCF margin is certainly high, we still use it as a basis. For growth, we assume 40% in fiscal 2026 and then assume growth will slow down over the years. In my opinion, this is a rather reasonable assumption, as the current high growth rates won’t last forever. When calculating with these still optimistic assumptions, we get an intrinsic value of $382.81 for Broadcom, making the stock slightly overvalued.

We can make the counterargument that Broadcom has been growing between 20% and 30% (when taking a 10-year rolling average) for a long time now, and this is certainly supporting the argument that growth won’t slow down to only 10% or even lower in 10 years from now. And while we should not ignore the risk that the spending boom will collapse at some point, the long history of high growth rates is a strong argument.

However, almost no company can grow 20% or 30% forever, and we should not bet on Broadcom being the outlier here. Additionally, while a terminal growth rate of 4% might seem extremely low, it is already the high end of the range the CFI is recommending. In my scenario, Broadcom would generate a free cash flow of $194 billion in ten years from now, which is clearly exceeding the free cash flow generation of every business in the world right now.

Of course, a lot can happen in 10 years, and we see in the chart above how the free cash flow of NVIDIA exploded in the last few years. But we should always keep in mind that we are calculating with extremely optimistic assumptions and are betting on a very unlikely scenario. And even when calculating with these very optimistic assumptions, Broadcom is still trading about 10% above its intrinsic value right now.

Conclusion

While I do not rule out a scenario where Broadcom will continue to grow its fundamental business at an extremely high pace (mid-double-digit growth), I remain very cautious about whether such an upside potential is reasonable.

As Broadcom is almost priced for perfection, even a small hiccup might send the stock down steeply. And if AI infrastructure spending should suddenly stop growing, Broadcom’s extremely high growth rates might vanish quickly.

Overall, I am rather bearish on the stock, and while the provided calculation is implying that Broadcom is close to its intrinsic value, I consider the assumptions that were made very optimistic and would rather argue to sell Broadcom at this point

data.title: Freedom Holding Remains A Compelling Buy Ahead (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
I Still Like FRHC's Prospects

My March 2021 article on Freedom Holding Corp. (FRHC) was actually my first piece ever written for the Seeking Alpha platform. It was bullish, and since then the stock has been up over 200%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) has managed to gain only ~85%, so I consider it a quite solid first research article. Since my most recent update on the stock, FRHC is up almost 20%, also beating the market for the same timeframe.

Anyway, as I keep my coverage, I can't ignore the recent financials and events, as well as the upcoming earnings release that I'd like to preview in today's update.

What we also can't ignore is the corporate development. Since 2021, Freedom has transformed from just a local Kazakhstani brokerage into a well-diversified multi-service provider with a much broader reach and addressable market, well beyond just the financial sector. I particularly like the execution. A few years ago, the management promised that they would focus on the SuperApp development, building the firm's banking, insurance, and non-financial solutions ecosystem around it. By the end of Freedom Holding's fiscal Q3 2026 (ended December 31, 2025, released in early February 2026), we've seen their banking segment having ~4.5 million clients, which was a 2 million client addition in just 3 quarters, primarily thanks to the SuperApp, because it recorded ~4.3 million users (it's one of the most downloaded apps in Kazakhstan).

The number of brokerage accounts increased by 21% during the third quarter of fiscal year 2026, reaching 828 000 compared to 683 000 as of March 31, 2025. The number of banking segment clients grew by 78%, reaching 4,4 million versus 2,5 million previously. The number of insurance segment clients stood at 1,2 million. Clients in other business segments increased by 15% to more than 700 000.

Source: The CEO's comments.

In a very short time, FRHC has managed to create an ecosystem that can retain many new clients who may have initially come to use non-banking services like Arbuz (grocery delivery) or Ticketon (online ticketing) but eventually became banking clients, using Freedom Holding's insurance (for cars or life) and taking loans to finance their in-app purchases, such as tickets or groceries.

Naturally, Freedom's SuperApp creates a sticky environment that effectively builds a sales loop, helping the firm increase its top line and balance sheet at a very fast pace. As of Q3, FRHC's total assets surged to ~$12.38 billion, which was a 25% increase since the beginning of this fiscal year. Revenues declined by 5.4% YoY, and the net income dropped by $76.2 million, which is -2.4% YoY. Despite this, I think it was justified given the client base expansion. In fact, the customer acquisition was solid. The main driver of the top-line contraction was a new KZ regulatory cap on agent commissions for policies associated with bank and microfinance loans. In addition to that, FRHC's new cashback-based loyalty program pushed down the banking revenues.

Operationally, I like what I saw in the Q3 print. FRHC showed $228.8 million in interest income (+2% YoY), with a huge addition to that from interest on customer loans - as the firm's portfolio keeps expanding, I think we'll likely see continued strength here in the next few quarters. I also liked to see FRHC's cost control staying in place - despite the general top-line fall, total expenses decreased to $534.7 million from $566.3 million (which is why EPS hasn't dropped that much). So, the regulatory changes and aggressive customer loyalty investments were dragging, but they were likely temporary headwinds that helped FRHC boost the client base. Now, the base is wide enough to start capitalizing on it.

In the next few years, I expect to see some margin expansion in FRHC's telecom segment. Freedom has been investing heavily in telecom and data centers' CapExes, and they are losing money for now. In the "other segments" item in their financials, we saw a net loss of $64.4 million for Q3 alone. But throughout 2026-2028, I think the optical lines and DCs will finally come to the monetization phase, and it's going to be recurring B2B and government enterprise revenues. Once this kicks in, the margins will rise from here, in my view.

Another medium-term catalyst that I see is FRHC's operations in Dubai (UAE). They've been building a strategic platform connecting global tech companies with their fintech ecosystem, and in the next couple of years, I expect that their SuperApp will enter new markets and expand globally (not just in Kazakhstan).

Now, let's talk about FRHC's upcoming earnings report.

It's hard to see what the analysts are projecting because, despite the fact that Freedom Holding is a $9 billion market cap fintech firm, it's not covered that much by the Street. Hence, no revenues/EPS consensus is available for today:

But I think the headline figures won't matter that much anyway. What's likely to matter more is the continuation of accounts growth. We need to see Freedom gain more users in its ecosystem, and I'm expecting to see their banking customers go toward the 5 million mark. On the CAPEX/OPEX sides, I can't be so certain, though, because the firm is still building the AI hub in partnership with NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA). But given that the balance sheet was strong in Q3, and the total assets amount exceeded $12 billion, I think FRHC will remain greatly profitable on the cash flow side.

In terms of valuation, FRHC's historic normalized P/E sits at 12-35x, which is a pretty wide range, but I think the forward valuation is likely to tilt toward the higher end here because FRHC isn't an ordinary bank, and it's not just a fintech firm anymore either. The diversification and the level of growth we're looking at demand a higher valuation multiple, in my view. So, if FRHC eventually comes back to its FY2024 EPS level in the next 2 years ($4.59 diluted normalized, per Seeking Alpha), and we see a forward growth of 20-25%, the P/E multiple might be close to 35x. In this case, my price target would come to almost $197/share:

$4.59 * 1.225 * 35x = $196.79/share.

Source: author's calculations.

This is an upside potential of 29%, so I view FRHC as an undervalued stock ahead of the earnings print.

Risks

The biggest risk to owning FRHC right now is the fact that the CEO, who is also the firm's founder, owns almost 70% of the outstanding shares. Usually, investors like it when the management has skin in the game, but in this case, the "skin" gives a risk of high ownership concentration that might lead to a liquidity shock when/if Mr. Turlov decides to sell at least 5% of his stake to the open market for whatever reason.

In addition to that, I should admit that my valuation model assumes two things that are very far from certain to date. First, I assume FRHC is capable of returning to its FY2024-like shape and reporting a similar EPS in 2027, plus a 20-25% increase. Second, I assume a relatively high P/E of 35x to account for FRHC's growth premium. If either of these assumptions fails to pan out, my price target could end up well below the current price.

Your Takeaway

I generally like the firm's ecosystem build-out and the ongoing transition to digital public infrastructure that looks visionary to me. I almost called the stock's local bottom in my most recent update, but FRHC is unlikely to lose its strong momentum just yet—the upcoming earnings report might reignite it. I see a 12–24 month upside potential of 29%, which is why I remain bullish on FRHC for now.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Lattice Semiconductor: Pricing In A Great Deal Of Future Earnings Is Risky
data.description:
Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC), a supplier of low-power programmable semiconductor chips like FPGAs, has been very hot lately. The stock has already appreciated by 66.9% YTD, with roughly half of those gains coming in the month of April with a gain of 32.4%, and that's with the month not yet over. The ongoing rally has spiked valuations, which have effectively priced in a large amount of future earnings growth, but that can become problematic since growth does not always come in as expected, especially when expectations have become so elevated, as seems to be the case for LSCC judging by the way it has rallied higher. In fact, while it may be tempting to ride the momentum higher in the stock, an argument can be made that the better move is to sit it out due to the risks involved in doing so.

LSCC Is Coming Off a Major Downturn

In my September 2022 article on LSCC, I mentioned how LSCC had done better than most in the industry, which had by then fallen victim to the industry downturn that caused sales and profits to go down for many semis. On the other hand, I also took note of the lack of progress the stock had made for the year, with LSCC basically going sideways during this time. In the end, I chose to rate LSCC a hold in the article.

A lot has happened in the almost four years that have passed. For instance, LSCC was able to stay clear of the industry downturn for a while longer, but it eventually caught up to LSCC. In fact, the last completed fiscal year, FY2025, and the preceding FY2024 showed the lingering effects of this downturn with major declines in the top and the bottom line compared to the peak of FY2022-2023.

The stock price was in the mid-fifties when the previous article was written four years ago, and as recently as mid-2025, LSCC was still trading below this price level. However, starting in the Summer of 2025, LSCC has come to life with a huge rally in the stock. The above chart shows how LSCC was trading in the forties one year ago, but it has racked up the gains in the past 12 months, especially more recently and in April in particular.

However, it is worth mentioning that after appreciating by about a third in value in less than a month, the stock is now in overbought territory with an RSI value in the seventies. Furthermore, if we connect all the highs during the rally, going back all the way to mid-2025, with a trendline, then LSCC is bumping up against this trendline. In other words, the conditions are there for a pullback or something more.

What is Driving the Rally in LSCC?

The chart posted earlier shows how the stock has done much better lately, something that would not have happened if a few things had not happened. First, LSCC got some good news in early 2025 when it released its Q4 FY2024 report because this report turned out to be the bottom in terms of the downturn, which had slashed quarterly results.

Revenue, operating profit, net profit, and so on all hit bottom that quarter. Still, both the FY2024 and FY2025 annual reports showed the negative impact of the lingering consequences of a drop in demand due to the downturn. FY2025 revenue of $523.26M, for instance, was slightly better than FY2024 revenue of $509.40M, but both are well below the peak of $737.15M in FY2023.

The bottom line was even worse off with GAAP EPS of $0.02 and $0.44 in FY2025 and FY2024, respectively, a fraction of the $1.85 LSCC earned in FY2023. In terms of non-GAAP, the drop-off was less severe, but earnings in FY2024 and FY2025 were still roughly half the level of FY2023. This shows what kind of impact the drop in demand had on LSCC.

Still, while the numbers have a ways to go to get back to where they were prior to the downturn, they have improved in recent quarters. Revenue and EPS, for instance, increased sequentially in every quarter in FY2025, although this was made possible in part by a lower base, which allowed for favorable comparisons.

More importantly, while the bottoming of the downturn certainly helped, it is no coincidence the stock really took off in the Summer of 2025 because that is when the semiconductor industry got a jolt from a number of high-profile companies like OpenAI, Oracle (ORCL), and Nvidia (NVDA) announcing they would pour huge sums of money into AI infrastructure, principally data centers.

LSCC got a boost from this because it too had upped its exposure to the AI boom by developing products specifically for the datacenters needed for AI. LSCC is best known for a wide range of FPGAs it offers, which includes those that can be used in network cards, storage cards, and other add-in cards for various applications like power control reporting.

Why the Possibility of a Miss Should Not Be Dismissed

LSCC also did its part during the summer to enable a rally because while the Q2 FY2025 report, which was released in August 2025, reported revenue that was flat to slightly down YoY, Q3 FY2025 guidance called for the first YoY increase in revenue since the downturn got underway in 2023. This return to growth in the top line was met with a 15.2% jump in the value of the stock after the report’s release.

However, it needs mentioning that LSCC has not been consistent with its quarterly reports during the rally. The next report in November triggered a 13.2% drop in the stock price after LSCC acknowledged risks to the recovery during the earnings call, including inventory issues, but the most recent, or Q4 FY2025 report in February triggered a 16.3% jump in the stock price after quarterly guidance, which covers Q1 FY2026, was well above expectations.

LSCC wrapped up FY2025 strong in February with non-GAAP EPS of $0.32 on revenue of $145.8M in Q4 FY2025, up from $0.20 on revenue of $117.4M in Q4 FY2024. These represent YoY gains of 60.0% and 24.2%, respectively, which support the notion LSCC has left the downturn behind. It also helped that server revenue increased by 85% in the report, which suggests data center demand due to AI is having an impact.

LSCC has yet to announce when it will release its Q1 FY2026 report, but based on past reports, the report is likely to come sometime in the first half of May. My estimate is that LSCC will announce non-GAAP EPS of $0.36 on revenue of $165M for Q1 FY2026, which would be much better than a year ago when LSCC announced non-GAAP EPS of $0.22 on revenue of $120.1M in Q1 FY2025.

Yet the risk that LSCC comes up short should not be dismissed based on what has happened after recent reports, which has been up and down. The last report was a beat, just as the one before was a miss, so if the recent pattern of alternating hits and misses holds, the upcoming report could be a miss. This miss may not necessarily be due to quarterly numbers but something else, like the earnings call suggesting growth may not be as fast as some seem to think based on how the stock price has been pushed higher.

Why the Risk of Disappointment Is There When Expectations Are Very High

Keep in mind that expectations are now much higher, especially after the rally in the stock price, which raises the possibility of coming up short. These elevated expectations can be seen in the valuations assigned to LSCC after the big rally in the stock price. In fact, while there are many semis out there that now trade at lofty valuations, LSCC is probably one of the more extreme ones.

For instance, LSCC has a market cap of $16.81B with TTM sales of $523.26M, which means LSCC is valued at 32.1x sales, which is far above the median of 3.6x in the sector. Other commonly referenced metrics are also on the lofty side. In terms of book value, LSCC is valued at a price-to-book of 23.5x, above the median of 3.9x. This includes cash and cash equivalents of $133.9M, which more than offset $42.1M in debt.

So LSCC is not any less expensive on the balance sheet than it is on the income statement. TTM non-GAAP EPS was $1.05, which accords LSCC a P/E ratio of 116.9x with the stock last closing at $122.80 a share. In terms of GAAP, which accounts for items left out by non-GAAP, valuations are even higher.

So anyone who is a buyer at these levels is, implicitly or explicitly, calling for not just any growth, but very fast growth ahead. This growth is needed to justify and bring down multiples. For instance, according to the methodology espoused by Peter Lynch, LSCC would need to grow non-GAAP earnings at a CAGR of 116.9% in the next 3-5 years in order to be deemed fairly valued relative to growth.

If LSCC wants to keep pace with this target, then FY2026 EPS would need to grow to $2.28, using FY2025 EPS of $1.05 as the base, unless it wants to have to grow even faster in following years. In comparison, the most optimistic projection on Wall Street calls for FY2026 EPS of $1.75, and most believe it will be much lower. If LSCC grows at a slower pace, LSCC is overvalued relative to growth.

FY2026 EPS of $2.28 would also surpass the high of $2.01 set in FY2023. It’s also worth mentioning that LSCC has no history of growing this fast. This does not mean LSCC cannot do much better than it ever has, but it still means the bar has been set very high as a result of the large increase in valuations, which raises the chance LSCC will disappoint.

LSCC Needs the AI Boom to Continue

LSCC is not alone in seeing its stock price soar higher, because many other semis have seen their stock price bid up with people looking to profit from the AI frenzy. While jumping in has paid off thus far in April, with many semis, including LSCC, posting huge gains, it is still prudent to be wary of the rally.

This is because while the AI boom is definitely still underway, there are signs that ought to give investors reason for pause. Elevated valuations are one, but the amount of capital companies are pouring into capex should also raise eyebrows, especially since it is negatively affecting the balance sheet of an increasing number of companies the longer it goes on, which includes large players like Oracle.

Another issue that needs to be watched is whether companies are following through on their announcements. The rally in the summer of 2025 was triggered by companies announcing mega deals, which included NVDA investing $100B in OpenAI, but since then the companies involved essentially backtracked by lowering the amounts to be invested.

A major catalyst for the semiconductor industry, which includes LSCC, has been the building of data centers, but this buildout is increasingly running into all sorts of problems. This includes shortages of parts and components to build these data centers, but also the impact these data centers have on the local community.

For instance, data centers consume huge amounts of electricity, which is driving up the cost for everyone else, and that is if there is enough for everyone. This has caused a backlash, with some communities protesting against data centers being built. This is one factor that has resulted in many data center projects being delayed, or worse.

If the building of data centers slows down for these and other reasons, LSCC may not grow as fast as it needs to warrant present elevated valuations. The recent stock rally is built on the assumption that LSCC is set to greatly increase revenue and profits due to AI demand, but the risk of the expected windfall coming in less is there.

Investor Takeaways

LSCC definitely has momentum on its side, and this alone could send the stock price even higher. However, the stock is now overbought after the recent rally, particularly in the month of April. LSCC is getting due for at least a pullback, especially since the stock is bumping up against the trendline that LSCC has respected since it started its move in the summer of 2025.

This also comes at a time when the next earnings report is getting due to be released, although no date has been announced. A lot is expected from LSCC, especially after the big rally of the past several weeks, but the issue here is that the risk of coming up short has also gone up as a consequence. LSCC has also been hit-and-miss in recent quarters, and recent patterns suggest a miss could be the one next in line.

The stock price has soared higher, but this has also pushed up valuations to lofty levels. This does not preclude LSCC from growing at a pace that warrants current valuations, but it does mean the bar has been set very high, which means that any hiccup, no matter how small, could be enough to cause the stock to surrender some or even all of the recent gains.

AI has been key to LSCC and the way the stock has behaved recently, and if LSCC is to live up to the growth current valuations demand from it, AI will need to stay strong. This means the building of new data centers needs to continue to ensure continued demand for LSCC, but there are nonetheless a number of developments that give reason to doubt whether that will happen, as elaborated on earlier in the article.

I am therefore neutral on LSCC with everything above in mind. The rally looks tempting, but there is a risk in pricing in a lot of future growth, which is what has happened after the rally in the stock. LSCC needs to grow very fast in future quarters just to live up to the elevated valuations and to keep the stock price elevated, because if it does not, it is a long way down after going as high as LSCC did.

It may be tempting to simply jump in and ride the momentum higher, but whether it is the very elevated valuations, an overbought stock at a time the upcoming earnings report has a high bar to cross, potential resistance in the chart posted earlier, or the problems facing the building of data centers, there is enough reason to avoid getting oneself in a position where the risk of a substantial haircut is present.

data.title: KNG: A Covered Call Dividend ETF With A Yield Target
data.description:
Fast Facts

First Trust Vest S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Target Income ETF (KNG) was launched on 3/26/2018 and tracks the Cboe S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Target Income Index Monthly Series. KNG magnifies income from dividend stocks by selling covered call options on these stocks. The fund has 138 holdings, a trailing 12-month yield of 8.58%, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.92%, and a total expense ratio of 0.74%. It is a large and liquid ETF, with $3.3 billion of AUM (assets under management) and an average daily dollar volume of $19 million. The sponsor, First Trust, is a financial services company based in Wheaton, Illinois, with over $300 billion in AUM. First Trust was one of the first asset management firms to offer smart beta and thematic ETFs.

Strategy

As described by First Trust, the underlying index is rules-based, with a primary objective to generate income approximately 8% over the dividend yield of the S&P 500 Index and a secondary objective of capital appreciation. For that purpose, the index includes an equal-weighted portfolio of the stocks contained in the S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Index with options traded on a U.S. exchange and monthly-rolled at-the-money short call options on each of these stocks. The portion of each stock position to be covered by a call option is dynamically adjusted to attain the income target. Such a covered call strategy enhances income with an option premium and also limits the gains from the stock portfolio.

The S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Index generally includes companies in the S&P 500 Index that have increased dividend payments each year for at least 25 consecutive years and meet certain market capitalization and liquidity requirements.

The equity index is rebalanced quarterly and reconstituted annually. The portfolio turnover rate was 152% in the most recent fiscal year and 92% in the previous year. This article will use as a benchmark the S&P 500 Index, represented by State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

Portfolio

KNG holds 69 stocks and the same number of positions in options, which all have the same expiration date next month. The portfolio has significant exposure in consumer staples (23.6% of asset value) and industrials (21.4%). Other sectors are below 13%. Compared to the S&P 500, KNG overweights consumer staples, industrials, materials, utilities, and real estate. It ignores communication services and greatly downplays technology and consumer discretionary.

The portfolio is diversified, with low company-specific risk. Stock positions are reset in equal weight every quarter. The current top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 17.2% of asset value, and the largest position is below 2%.

KNG has the second-highest expense ratio and is in last position for total return and Sharpe ratio (a measure of risk-adjusted performance) since November 2023, making it uncompelling compared to peers.

Takeaway

KNG aims at a yield of 8% over the S&P 500’s from a covered call strategy in dividend aristocrats. KNG is well-suited for investors seeking a predictable yield from options, but those prioritizing risk-adjusted and total returns may prefer DIVO, SPYI, and GPIX. Please also note that the total return profile is inclusive of the income, so investors who choose to distribute the income should also take note of its price return.

In periods of higher growth for the underlying securities, KNG is likely to experience NAV drag as a result of the calls written against its holdings. In a flat or range-bound environment, the total return may look more compelling than the underlying holdings on their own (this is the most favorable trading environment for this ETF). However, as the underlying securities experience a downturn, KNG's price action would follow. Its income component would cushion the total return to the degree of income generated, but there is no structural floor for this ETF through an options strategy (for instance, no long puts).

  • Pros: target income, low company risk, moderate sector risk.
  • Cons: high fees, lags behind competitors.

This article answers these three main questions about KNG:

  1. What is KNG's objective, and what are its holdings?
  2. How does KNG compare to other enhanced income ETFs?
  3. What type of strategy is KNG best suited for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Market Intraday Outlook - The Tech Honeymoon Is Over... Right Before Earnings?
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The honeymoon phase for US equities is officially facing its first major stress test in weeks.

The Nasdaq is currently leading US stock benchmarks to the downside, as traders scramble to unroll risk ahead of the most critical corporate mega-cap earnings stretch of the year.

Compounding the anxiety from the releases is a still quite unstable and uncertain geopolitical landscape.

While recent reports suggest that Iran is expected to submit a revised peace proposal soon—desperate to escape the economic chokehold of the US naval blockade—the timeline for an actual resolution remains cloudy.

This morning, President Trump posted in a Truth Social bomb that Tehran is actively demanding the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, yet until a concrete deal is signed, the global logistical nightmare persists.

Adding to the supply drought, WTI crude oil has surged right back around and above the $100 mark with the UAE officially exiting from OPEC.

This resurgence in energy commodities is hurting broader stock market sentiment and adding pressure to the recent bullish momentum – Check out our recent WTI piece to learn more on these issues!

Prediction-market odds for a proper peace deal have slowly crumbled, going up above 70% but are now back below 35% – So remaining hopeful in such an environment could be quite risky.

On the corporate front, the underlying US economy continues to show immense resilience, highlighted by a strong earnings beat from Coca-Cola (KO) this morning and keeping the DJIA afloat. Today's after-the-bell lineup, featuring Visa (V) and T-Mobile (TMUS), will keep traders busy, but it is merely an appetizer.

However, the quintessential challenge for Wall Street arises tomorrow when four of the Magnificent 7 heavyweights—Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META), Google (GOOG)(GOOGL), and Microsoft (MSFT)—release their Q1 earnings.

With tech valuations stretched to the absolute limit in a flash melt-up since the ceasefire announcement and the macroeconomic backdrop clouded by triple-digit oil, these titans must deliver flawless guidance to prevent a vicious, broader market correction.

Let's dive into intraday charts and trading levels for the Dow Jones Industrial Average, Nasdaq Composite, and S&P 500.

Current Session's Stock Heatmap

Except for Nvidia (NVDA) and Microsoft still extending their remarkable rebounds (having underperformed throughout early 2026 trading), the entire market is suffering from the latest fundamental flows – Only Energy and Financials maintain a balanced price action.

Dow Jones 2H Chart and Trading Levels

The Dow is still holding its super-strong 500-point range between 49,000 and 49,500 – Keep these levels in check for the breakout trading.

Normally, consolidating near all-time highs is a positive sign as overbought conditions ease and the market rebalances while not showing any sign of weakness, so this would add to higher odds of a bullish breakout.

Nonetheless, the current fundamentals are very fragile, and any bad news in the Middle East could see a flash drop lower – Worsening sentiment hasn't hurt the price action yet.

data.title: SPGI: The February Bears Were Wrong (Earnings Review)
data.description:
Introduction

S&P Global (SPGI) is in the portfolios of many super investors. Although I am in no way close to being compared to this category, the stock is present in both of my portfolios. Therefore, I have held through a period of time during which the stock has been an underperformer and has been hit by two main issues: AI disruption fears regarding the S&P Market Intelligence division and the Mobility division spin-off. I tackled both of these issues in my previous coverage when I ran a detailed head-to-head comparison between S&P Global and Moody's (MCO). In particular, the Q4 2025 earnings report released in February 2026 sent the stock down by almost 12% as the FY26 EPS guidance came in roughly 2% below the then-consensus of $19.96. This is why the Q1 report was something I was waiting for, as it would have given me the first test to understand whether the sell-off was a correct reaction or not. So, let's look at the report and see if the stock continues to be an interesting opportunity or not.

S&P Global's Q1 2026 Earnings Report

To make sure we are all on board, let me quickly recap how S&P Global is structured. It currently has five divisions: ratings, market intelligence, indices, energy, and mobility. Ratings assigns credit ratings, and it is the division most people know about, together with indices, which is the business that licenses the S&P 500 and many other important benchmarks. As far as Market Intelligence goes, it is the platform that distributes analytics and financial data together with workflow tools, such as Capital IQ Pro, Visible Alpha, and RatingXpress. This is the business that investors are worried about, as one narrative that is being repeatedly heard believes that AI will turn this key business into an unmeaningful one (the idea is that AI can pull financial data on its own, without paying thousands of dollars per subscription plan to S&P Global). Energy provides data about the commodity markets, and Mobility does more or less the same with the automotive market.

We should understand that these businesses are not just juxtaposed one next to the other. Ratings and indices build what I would call a financial data infrastructure. After all, most market participants have to use the data either by mandate or by regulation. The other businesses, such as market intelligence, energy, and mobility, are built on top of the first two. As a matter of fact, S&P Global gathers so much data about companies and the economy that it can sell it to those who need to have more insight into the energy and automotive industries. Although it is true that if you use an LLM to pull financial data from SEC filings, the results are almost completely correct, there is something that LLMs can't provide: accountability. Subscribers to S&P Global's services and products need a data provider that can be accountable if a mistake is found. Secondly, it could sound counterintuitive, but actually, some LLMs are becoming distribution partners of S&P Global's Market Intelligence. This shifts the threat from the data to the interface. If AI can sit on top of reliable data, the tools that S&P Global provides (i.e., the Capital IQ Pro dashboards) could see more competitive pressure from AI assistants. This is rather important because Market Intelligence makes money not only by selling data but also by selling tool subscriptions. The best-case scenario could then be described as coopetition. S&P continues licensing Market Intelligence data and gains from incremental distribution but, at the same time, sees more pressure on the workflow tools pricing with a mild margin compression as the final outcome. The worst scenario is that AI commoditizes financial data access. A scenario in the middle would be that workflow commoditization increases, and this means that some enterprises will replace their costly Capital IQ Pro subscription ($35k/seat) with a Claude Pro subscription ($20/month). Here, churn would be a concern, and MI revenue growth would surely slow down.

Let's then start the earnings review from the MI division. In Q1, subscription growth was at 6%, with stable margins. This tilts the situation toward the first scenario (coopetition), but it doesn't rule out the outcomes outlined by the other two scenarios. I think that we will need to watch for the 4% threshold: if MI's growth is below this, then we would need to start thinking that AI is indeed a threat to subscriptions.

The question, however, remains, but I think that we should not frame it in a catastrophic way, such as "Will AI destroy S&P Global?" This is misleading. As investors, we have to focus on who is going to capture the value between data and analysts. So far, it has been S&P Global, and the company still claims it will. But so does Anthropic, for example.

Having said this, let's look at the overall performance of the company.

SPGI reported revenue growth of 10% to $4.2B, which was a beat of 2.2% vs. estimates. Its EPS also topped consensus, coming in at $4.97, 3.1% above the expected $4.82. The company's adj. operating margin increased by a sound 100 bps to 51.8%, which proves, once again, that S&P Global is truly efficient and able to generate billions in operating cash every year. Subscription revenues grew by 6% to $2B. So, the beat was driven by transaction revenue, which was up by 15% YoY to $980M. This was the most important number in the report, which shows that there could be some issues down the road if the cycle slows down and elevated issuance doesn't go on.

Looking at the segment breakdown, we see that Ratings was the first revenue stream, with a weight of 31.2%, while Market Intelligence was second with a 31.1% contribution to the company's top line. Their profitability is different: Ratings reached adj. operating margins of 68% (+160 bps YoY), while Market Intelligence is at 34% (+80 bps YoY). Energy is the third contributor, with a weight of 15.6%, with adj. operating margins at 49% (+120 bps YoY). Indices is the fourth leg, with a 12.4% weight and a margin structure at 74% (+90 bps YoY). In fifth place, we have Mobility with a 10.9% weight and margins at 40% (+150 bps YoY). All of this adds up to a margin expansion of 100 bps to 52%.

This report also addresses another concern investors had, in addition to AI. When the company reported a quarter ago, some feared that the modest guidance implied that the ratings transaction cycle had peaked. Look at the numbers below. We see that transaction revenue increased 15% YoY. For those who are not familiar with this part of the company, transaction revenue is the revenue that is generated when debt is issued. There is then a surveillance fee that is recurring, which is called non-transaction revenue, which was up 11% YoY. Combine the two, and we have ratings that grew by 13% YoY, taking the lead as the most important revenue stream of the company in Q1. The adj. operating margin was up by 160 bps.

Since the prior guidance and the newly released full-year guidance maintain growth for this segment between 4% and 7%. There are then two possible explanations: favorable issuance conditions in Q1 or conservative guidance. Once the earnings transcript is out, we might know a bit more, but I tilt toward the second framing.

Looking at the other segments, Indices is no surprise: high margins, revenue growth of 17% YoY, with asset-linked fees up 18% YoY to $339M, while exchange-traded derivatives royalties, which are volume driven, were up 20% YoY to $96M. Since Q1 was volatile, S&P Global benefited because derivative hedging activity usually increases.

Energy and Mobility reported revenue growth of 7% and 8%, respectively. Since the commodity markets were very active in Q1, it is no surprise to see that usage-based sales royalties were up by 27% to $37M. We also recently learned that SPGI wants to divest from its geoscience and petroleum engineering software portfolio. It is another step toward becoming a company more thoroughly focused on ratings, indices, and data analytics. Mobility did well, but the pending spin-off will be presented in more detail on May 12, 2026. This could be a key near-term catalyst for the whole company because it will enable investors to understand the path for the two companies after this deconsolidation is carried out.

Turning toward the company's cash flows and capital allocation policies, we see that SPGI generated $920M in FCF, repurchased shares for $1B, and paid $288M in dividends. The outlook is that the company will return 100% of its FCF in 2026. Over the TTM month, S&P Global has reduced its share count by 3.3%, which is a way to build an EPS tailwind and take advantage of the current softness of the stock.

The guidance didn't change, if not by a 30 bps reduction in the revenue growth ceiling, which is now seen at 8.3% instead of 8.6%. This will be mainly due to FX.

S&P Global's Valuation

From what I see, this print breaks the bearish narrative that has hovered over the stock since February. The market seems to be thinking that this could be a correct reading, as the stock is up by 3% after earnings, while the market is down by 1%. Ratings is ahead of the FY guidance, and Indices is a powerhouse. Soon, the Mobility spin-off will become clearer. The question is the durability of the revenue growth for the Ratings' division and the scenarios we discussed above regarding MI.

Currently, the stock trades at 23x its 2026 EPS. It might not be extremely cheap, but the market's average is currently between 20x and 21x. Considering that we are talking about a company that is part of an oligopoly with margins above 50% that rarely stumbles or fails to deliver even amidst market volatility and an uncertain macroeconomic environment, we are talking about a stock that is in a buy zone. I believe that as long as the stock stays below a 25x multiple, S&P Global continues to be attractive. As a result, I stick to my buy rating.

data.title: Buy The Dip: 9-13% Yields Getting Dirt Cheap
data.description:
I love buying dividend stocks that go on deep discounts, especially when the underlying fundamentals are very strong. The reason for this is that not only do I get a very attractive current income stream that can accelerate my path towards financial independence, but I can also potentially retire early. Dividend income frees me from being dependent on market volatility, and it also gives me significant long-term total return performance potential, due to the fact that you can combine high current yield with some growth potential and then valuation multiple expansion potential as the market eventually realizes that it has been way too bearish on these securities.

With this in mind, in this article, I'm going to discuss two opportunities that yield between 9% and 13% that I think have gotten way too cheap on their recent pullbacks.

A Deep-Discount BDC Hiding in Plain Sight

The first opportunity we're going to discuss is Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC). OBDC is a business development company (BIZD) managed by Blue Owl Capital (OWL) and is focused on direct lending to U.S. middle market companies. It currently trades at a whopping 24% discount to its net asset value and offers a very juicy 13.1% dividend yield. The company recently had a large slice of its marks validated due to a $1.4 billion sale at around par value of Blue Owl loans that included a significant portion that were held at OBDC. Additionally, OBDC recently issued a considerable number of investment-grade corporate bonds that were entirely bought out by PIMCO, which is another vote of confidence in the fund's financial strength.

This is also seen with its relatively conservative posture, with 79% of the portfolio invested in senior secured loans, including 73.1% of the portfolio in senior secured loans. It is also well diversified across 234 portfolio companies spread across 30 industries, and its top 10 positions only make up 23% of its fair value. Not only that, but its non-accruals only come at 1.1% at fair value and 2.3% at cost, which are very competitive with many of its BDC peers, like Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC). The lion's share of the portfolio is performing well, with 90.7% of it rated either 1 or 2 and only 1.4% of it rated 4 or 5 on the risk rating scale, meaning that it has a limited risk of a significant ballooning in its non-accruals.

The company also has 12% of the portfolio invested in specialty finance and joint venture opportunities that are generating very high returns on equity of 14% plus, which helped to offset headwinds from recent Federal Reserve rate cuts that have reduced its net investment income. Meanwhile, it does have significant software (IGV) loan exposure; however, its loan-to-value ratio on its software loans is around 30%, which gives it strong downside protection even if these software businesses do suffer due to AI disruption.

The balance sheet is also in sound shape, with it recently being upgraded to Baa2 by Moody's back in January, which gives it one of the highest credit ratings in the entire BDC sector. It has $4.2 billion in total liquidity, with a weighted average term to maturity of 4.3 years, with most of its maturities laddered to 2029 and beyond, and a debt-to-equity of 1.19 times, below its 1.25 times target ceiling, which gives it room to maneuver to potentially add additional leverage to either buy back stock at a steep discount to NAV or invest in attractive opportunities that come its way. In fact, the company has been executing aggressively on share buybacks. It bought back $148 million worth of stock in Q4, which delivered a substantial 5 cents per share NAV accretion for the quarter. It recently expanded its authorization to $300 million, which gives it significant flexibility to continue buying back stock at an aggressive clip.

Navigating Dividend Cut Risk

Perhaps one of the biggest cons to OBDC right now is that it is likely going to cut its dividend. Its base dividend of 37 cents per share slightly exceeded net investment income of 36 cents per share this past quarter, and the company will likely continue to face some headwinds from interest rate changes as well as some potential non-accruals. However, buybacks are a way that it can help offset some of this headwind. Nevertheless, I do expect them to trim their dividend by 10 to 20% to create a more sustainable floor to the dividend and likely will add a supplemental dividend on top of that, as it has done in the past and as peers like Golub Capital BDC (GBDC) have done recently.

There are reasons to like it, however, beyond just the strong underlying fundamentals and the deep discount. For example, insiders have been buying fairly aggressively over the past six months, and management has reiterated that buybacks are an attractive use of capital. The fact that it is actually buying back stock aggressively is a strong signal, in contrast to many other externally managed peers like FS KKR Capital Corp. (FSK), which have not been, despite trading at even larger discounts to NAV. Given that OBDC is externally managed, the management team therefore has little incentive to buy back stock, given that it reduces the fees that they earn from the BDC and takes valuable permanent capital off the table. A company like this that buys back stock so aggressively is sending a very powerful signal that it has high conviction in the inherent value of its portfolio.

OBDC is certainly not risk-free, as there's always the chance that it could suffer a small sell-off in the wake of a dividend cut (though this has likely already been priced in, as evidenced by how other BDCs have responded to similar dividend cuts in the recent past). Additionally, if OBDC sees an uptick in non-accruals, that would certainly at least provide some justification for the market's bearishness on it. However, the discount now has gotten so large, especially relative to the current fundamentals of the company, that this appears to be overblown in my view. Thus, I view it as a strong buy on the recent sell-off.

Energy at a Price the Market Got Wrong

The other opportunity we're going to discuss is Alliance Resource Partners (ARLP), which is a K1-issuing LP that is involved in the coal, oil, and gas royalty business. It generates the majority of its revenue from coal, although it has a rapidly growing oil and gas royalties business that is helping to diversify its income away from coal, giving the company a much longer runway than the market appears to be currently giving it credit for. Additionally, investors can collect a tax-advantaged income yield that is near 10%. While near-term revenues have declined slightly year-over-year due to the roll-off of legacy contracts locked in the immediate aftermath of the Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022, the business is positioned to inflect towards growth moving forward, as its coal business is 95% contracted for the year and is benefiting from strong demand for base power to support the data center build-out across the country.

Additionally, its oil and gas royalty business is benefiting from the elevated energy pricing in the wake of the Iran war and Hormuz Strait closure. When combined with aggressive acquisitions and strong returns on investment that the company is generating in the oil and gas royalty business, it seems likely that ARLP is going to continue growing its cash flow over time. Moreover, the balance sheet is in phenomenal shape, with a Net Leverage Ratio of just 0.69 times and $431.2 million in liquidity. Finally, if you include the around $45 million worth of Bitcoin on the balance sheet, the Net Leverage Ratio falls considerably further, and the liquidity for the company is considerably higher.

Why Risks Are Overblown and Its Upside Is Overlooked

Of course, ARLP does have risks stemming from its significant coal exposure and the strong political opposition to coal by elements of the Democratic Party, which polls indicate will likely have a strong showing in the upcoming midterm elections. However, strong data center-fueled demand for affordable base power, as well as ARLP's robust growth in its natural gas and oil royalties business, helps to mitigate these threats. This is also aided by its very strong balance sheet. Overall, between its near 10% dividend distribution yield and its increasing diversification and growth prospects, along with its strong balance sheet, I think ARLP is a fairly attractive buy at its recent dip, especially given the changing global dynamics that make U.S. energy more attractive than ever.

The Two-Stock Income Engine That Could Supercharge Your Path to Early Retirement

OBDC and ARLP investors can get pretty broad diversification with exposure to energy and natural resources on the one hand, while getting exposure to a broadly diversified portfolio of senior secured debt investments on the other. On a combined basis, you get a very attractive income yield along with significant capital appreciation potential as well. As a result, I rate both of these opportunities as attractive buys and exactly the type of opportunities we look to capitalize on in our 7 to 8% yielding, total return-outperforming, below-market beta portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Amazon Stock Faces A Post-Earnings Unwind
data.description:
Amazon.com, Inc.’s (AMZN) stock has surged nearly 30% since late March to an all-time high. The setup into earnings suggests that the move is at risk of being unwound quickly.

While the majority of investors will focus on whether results were good or bad, what may matter more is that options positioning heading into these results is overwhelmingly bullish, and the implied volatility is very high. Positioning in the options market may determine the stock's direction.

Solid Results

The company is expected to post strong results on April 29th, with first-quarter revenue rising 13.9% to $177.3 billion and earnings rising 3% to $1.64 per share. CapEx, on the other hand, is expected to surge by 65.5% to $41.4 billion. For the full year, CapEx is expected to rise 44% to $189.8 billion.

Investors will pay close attention to Amazon Web Services, or AWS, which is expected to grow by 25.1% to $36.6 billion in the first quarter and by 25.9% to $38.9 billion in the second quarter.

AWS growth and CapEx spending are clearly the two most important line items for investors when reviewing the results. Unless results materially exceed expectations, the unwind in implied volatility and positioning is likely to dominate the price action.

Implied Volatility Is Very High

Implied volatility for $260 strike price call options expiring on May 1 is currently about 98%, and the premium for that call option is around $9.50 per contract. The buyer of the call needs the stock to trade above $270 per share to start earning a profit following the results, a gain of about 4%.

Once the company reports results and the implied falls, which in Amazon's case have been significant and have historically fallen back into the 30 to 40% range, the call options may only be worth about $3. If the stock is trading below $260 following the results, the options would essentially be worthless.

The decline in IV doesn't just happen in the one-option series; it happens across the chain for both puts and calls because implied volatility affects all option premiums. However, because overall options positions are overly bullish, there will be more decay in call premiums than in put premiums.

Premium Decay

That will have negative impacts on the stock price, the first being that all calls above $260 will decay in value, and the call holders will lose money unless the stock rises above $270. But more importantly, there is a very large amount of call delta built up at $270, so if the stock can't clear $270, those call options, and the options priced higher, are likely to be sold by current holders.

It also means that market makers will find themselves over-hedged relative to the call positioning and will have stock to sell following the results as premium values decline.

The gamma profile suggests a lot of resistance built up in the stock between $260 and $275, with call gamma very heavy. Meanwhile, because the stock has so much call gamma exposure, market-maker hedging flows are likely to go against Amazon's rising, suggesting that market makers are sellers of the stock on rallies and buyers on dips.

Technical View

The technical chart does reveal much of the same information as the option gamma positioning. Currently, the $260 area is serving as both support and resistance. Meanwhile, with a break of support, the next support level comes at the gap created on April 14 at $240, with more support down at another gap from April 8 at $220.

With the stock extended, implied volatility elevated, and call positioning crowded, the setup favors a post-earnings unwind. Without a clear upside surprise, the path of least resistance is lower.

data.title: Seanergy Maritime: The Capesize Pureplay Trading At A Substantial Discount And With Rising Dividends
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Seanergy Maritime Holdings Corp. (SHIP) is a capesize pure play, perfectly positioned to benefit from the rise in rates. The company’s dividend policy should substantially increase the dividends it pays, helping to reduce the valuation gap with its NAV and peers.

Background

Seanergy Maritime is a drybulk company, one of the only two public pure plays in the capesize segment.

Right now its fleet consists of 20 Capesizes with an average age of above 14 years; however, it has sold two of its oldest 2010 Capesizes with delivery in the next few months. To further reduce its fleet age, the company had 3 newbuilds with delivery starting in 2027, and it has recently added two newbuilds with deliveries scheduled for 2027 as well.

Seanergy was founded in 2008 and filed for Chapter 11 in 2012 after the financial crisis collapse. Stamatis Tsantanis took over as CEO in 2012 and restructured the company via an equity raise and still owns about 10% of the company. In 2022, Seanergy spun off its oldest vessel and created United Maritime Corporation (USEA). However, despite some market distrust for this movement and some previous capital raises, management has behaved correctly since then. In fact, they have increased the fleet by 80% since 2020, and since the new dividend policy was established in 2022, they have paid more than $50 million in dividends. The dividend policy was updated in 2024:

And while it states that the company will pay 50% of its operating cash flow, in reality, it is a discretionary dividend because it has a discretionary quarterly reserve.

Besides the CEO’s stake, other Greek investors have significant ownership, like Costamare Inc. (CMRE) CEO, with around a 10% stake, and George Economou’s stake of around 9%. In fact, Economou’s stake prompted a poison pill by Seanergy in December 2023 to protect against a takeover offer well below NAV.

The company charters most of its vessels on index-linked time charters with the possibility to fix them based on the FFA curve. This explains why Seanergy’s realized rates are really close to the BCI.

In the graph above, one can note that Q1 is almost always the softest month of the year; however, this Q1 has been strong. In fact, I expect the company’s Q1 realized rate to be slightly above $24,500, not far from its $26,600 from Q4. In fact, Q2 has started with an important surge, which is a good indication for the rest of the year:

Financial Position and Valuation

Seanergy has a strong financial position with $62 million in cash and $290 million in debt, which is much lower than its vessels’ value, which is $506 million in book value and around $800 million in market value.

Moreover, despite the newbuilds, with the two vessel sales and strong market rates, I don’t expect leverage to increase meaningfully.

The company has full exposure to the capesize rates, implying significant upside if rates remain strong:

For reference, the strongest year for the company was 2024, when it achieved $98.4 million, and this year is poised to break that.

As mentioned above, I already expect a strong Q1 given management guidance of 77% of days fixed at $24,700 per day on average and the rates evolution since then. My Q1 earnings estimate is slightly above $0.5 EPS, higher than analysts’ estimates:

I also expect that the company will maintain its Q4 dividend of $0.2, with further increases in the future if earnings rise as expected.

Regarding valuation, the company is trading at a substantial discount to NAV, around 50%.

While the company has historically traded at a significant discount, I believe that if the dry bulk market is as strong as the futures suggests:

I believe that the company could pay close to $1.5 per share in dividends for 2026. That implies a forward yield of about 10%, higher than usual. Assuming a 7% yield would imply a fair value of around $21, or 0.75x NAV, which I consider correct.

Risks

There are two main risks. The first one is that rates disappoint. This will reduce market values, and dividends will be low, making it difficult to rerate higher. If vessel values move down and the discount is maintained or even increases, the stock could trade substantially lower than current levels.

The second risk I see is the discretionary quarterly reserve in the dividend calculation. If the company decides to increase the discretionary reserve and not pay substantial dividends, the discount will be difficult to close.

Conclusion

Seanergy Maritime is the only capesize pure play trading at a substantial discount to NAV at a time when rates are the highest they have been in Q1 for many years, and there are good prospects for the year.

With the company’s exposure to the capesize index, it is perfectly positioned to benefit from such rates. If those rates translate to higher dividends, I expect the stock to rerate higher.
